name: Poltergeist
description: A poltergeist, derived from the German words 'poltern' meaning 'to make a noise' and 'geist' meaning 'spirit,' is a type of supernatural entity responsible for physical disturbances that are often attributed to mischievous or malevolent intent. Unlike ghosts, which are believed to be the spirits of deceased individuals, poltergeists are not typically thought to be spirits of the dead. Instead, they are often theorized to be manifestations of repressed psychological trauma or subconscious energy, particularly in adolescents experiencing emotional turmoil. Their activity can range from minor annoyances like moving objects and creating noises to more dramatic events such as throwing furniture, starting fires, and even physical attacks. The phenomenon is often associated with specific locations or individuals, and the activity tends to be sporadic and unpredictable, making it difficult to study scientifically. The belief in poltergeists is widespread across cultures and has been documented for centuries, though explanations vary widely from paranormal to psychological.
subtitle: Noisy and disruptive spirit
appearance: Poltergeists are typically invisible, lacking a physical form. Their presence is indicated by their actions and the effects they have on their environment rather than by any visual manifestation. Some accounts suggest they can briefly manifest as shadows or wisps of energy during periods of intense activity.
habitat: Poltergeist activity is often localized to specific buildings, particularly homes or places where emotional distress is prevalent. They are not tied to specific geographical locations but rather to environments conducive to their manifestation.
behavior: Poltergeists are characterized by disruptive and often chaotic behavior. Their actions range from minor pranks to more destructive acts, often seemingly motivated by mischief or malevolence. The activity is typically unpredictable and can fluctuate in intensity. Many believe their behavior is linked to the emotional state of individuals in the affected environment, particularly adolescents or those experiencing stress or trauma.

keyMyths.title: The Bell Witch
keyMyths.description: The Bell Witch of Adams, Tennessee, is one of the most famous poltergeist cases in American folklore. From 1817 to 1821, the Bell family was reportedly tormented by an unseen entity that pinched, slapped, and verbally abused them. The entity was attributed to a witch named Kate Batts, who held a grudge against John Bell. The story has been passed down through generations and remains a popular tale of poltergeist activity. The case is documented in multiple books and historical accounts.
keyMyths.source: Ingram, Martin V. An Authenticated History of the Bell Witch. Red River Press, 1894.

keyMyths.title: The Enfield Poltergeist
keyMyths.description: The Enfield Poltergeist case in London, England, between 1977 and 1979, involved alleged poltergeist activity in the home of the Hodgson family. The disturbances included furniture moving, objects being thrown, and strange noises. The case gained widespread attention and was investigated by paranormal researchers, including Maurice Grosse and Guy Lyon Playfair. Despite skepticism, the Enfield Poltergeist remains one of the most well-documented and debated poltergeist cases in history.
keyMyths.source: Playfair, Guy Lyon. This House is Haunted: The True Story of the Enfield Poltergeist. White Crow Books, 2011.

keyMyths.title: The Rosenheim Poltergeist
keyMyths.description: In 1967, the law office of Sigmund Adam in Rosenheim, Germany, experienced unexplained phenomena, including lights swinging, telephones ringing without being dialed, and filing cabinets opening and closing. Physicist Hans Bender investigated the incidents and concluded that they were indeed paranormal in nature. The case remains controversial, but it is one of the most extensively studied poltergeist cases in Europe, documented with physical evidence and witness testimonies.
keyMyths.source: Bender, Hans. Neue Parapsychologische Studien. J.A. Barth, 1981.
